Title: How to Configure a Print Server Introduction: A print server is a device or software that allows multiple computers to share one or more printers over a network. By configuring a print server, you can centralize printer management, reduce clutter, and save money by sharing expensive high-volume printers among several users. This tutorial will guide you through setting up a dedicated print server using Windows Server 2019 as an example. However, most steps are applicable to other operating systems with minor adjustments. **Part I: Setting Up Your Hardware** Step 1: Choose your hardware ------------------------------ To set up a physical print server, choose a computer (desktop or laptop) running an appropriate operating system (such as Windows Server, Linux, or macOS). Make sure this machine has enough processing power and memory to handle printing tasks efficiently without causing bottlenecks. Additionally, select a compatible printer connected via USB or Ethernet cable. Key tip: For best results, consider purchasing Network Attached Storage (NAS) devices specifically designed for print serving duties since they offer better performance, reliability, and energy efficiency compared to generic PCs. Step 2: Install the printer --------------------------- Connect your chosen printer to the future print server via its designated port (USB or Ethernet), then turn on both devices. On the print server, install the latest drivers provided by the manufacturer's website if necessary. Key guideline: Always download and install updates before connecting any peripherals like printers to ensure compatibility and security. Step 3: Share the printer ------------------------- On the print server, open **Control Panel > Devices and Printers**. Right-click on your newly installed printer and select **"Printer properties"**, followed by the **"Sharing"** tab. Check the box next to *"Share this printer"* and provide a share name. Click **"Apply"** and **"OK"** to confirm changes. ![Windows Printer Sharing](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ae/Configure-a-Print-Server-Step-3-Version-4.jpg/aid578687-v4- Why don't you use a pencil too? Electrons take part in gravitational, electromagnetic and weak interactions. The electromagnetic force is strongest in common situations. Electrons repel (push apart) from each other because they have the same electric charge. Electrons are attracted to protons because they have opposite electric charge. An electron has an electric field, which describes these forces. The electricity that powers televisions, motors, mobile phones, and many other things is actually many electrons moving through wires or other conductors. Title: How to Draw a Pokeball in Microsoft Paint Drawing a Pokeball in Microsoft Paint is an easy and fun way to create your own Pokemon-themed artwork. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating a simple yet accurate representation of this iconic item. **Step 1: Open Microsoft Paint** Firstly, locate the Microsoft Paint application on your computer. It can usually be found under the 'Accessories' or 'Windows Accessories' folder within the Start menu or Apps list. Once located, click on its icon to launch the program. **Step 2: Set Up Your Canvas** After opening Microsoft Paint, you'll see a blank white canvas. You may adjust the size of this canvas by clicking on the "Resize" button in the Home tab. For our purpose, let's keep it at the default size (10 x 10 inches). **Step 3: Create the First Half of the Circle** In the Home tab, ensure that the color black is selected as your foreground color. Then, pick the 'Ellipse' tool from the Shapes section. Click and drag diagonally across the canvas to start drawing half of a circle. Release the mouse button when you reach the desired diameter - about two-thirds of the height of the canvas should suffice. **Step 4: Complete the Full Circle** To complete the other half of the circle, we need to copy and flip the existing shape. To do so, first select the 'Select' tool from the Tools section. Now carefully draw a selection around the semi-circle, making sure not to include any part of the background. Right-click anywhere inside the selection area and choose 'Copy'. Next, move your cursor away from the original shape, then right-click again and select 'Paste'. A second semicircle identical to the first one will appear. Using the 'Selection' tool once more, align this new shape directly below the initial one until they form a full circle. **Step 5: Adding Color** Now it's time to add some color! Select red from the Colors panel as your foreground color. Choose the 'Fill with color' option from the Image section of the Home tab. Click anywhere inside the lower half of the circle; it should now turn red. Repeat this process using white as your foreground color for the top portion of the circle. Remember to always select 'Fill with color' before clicking within the boundaries of the respective sections. **Step 6: Creating the Button** Switch back to black as your foreground color. From the Tools section, grab the 'Rectangle' tool. Position your cursor near the intersection point between the halves, just outside the boundary line of the white sector. Drag downwards to draw a small rectangle covering approximately a quarter of the vertical space. This creates the metallic button often seen in Pokeball designs. If needed, use the 'Select' tool to fine-tune the dimensions or positioning of the rectangle. **Step 7: Finalizing the Design** For added detail, switch to grey as your foreground color. Grab the 'Line' tool from the Tools section and set its thickness to 1 pixel in the Image section of the Home tab. Draw horizontal lines above and beneath the button to represent dividers. And there you have it! With these steps followed correctly, you've successfully drawn a Pokeball using Microsoft Paint. Save your masterpiece by going to File > Save As... and choosing a suitable location and file name. Mommy can you give me some blocks to put over in my bag? Once upon a time, there was a little bird named Tweety. Tweety was a happy bird, but he had a big problem. He loved to chew on feathers, but he couldn't find any feathers to chew on! One day, Tweety saw a big feather on the ground. He was so happy that he started to chew on it right away. But then, something strange happened. Tweety became very grumpy. He didn't know why he felt that way. Later that day, Tweety saw a sign that said "Don't chew on feathers, they can make you grumpy!" Tweety felt silly for not reading the sign before. From that day on, Tweety never chewed on feathers again and he was always happy. The end. I'm going to build it way, way up if Mommy will let me have some. Title: How to Decorate a Chain Link Fence for Christmas ====================================================== A chain link fence may seem like an unlikely candidate for holiday decoration, but with a little creativity, it can become a festive addition to your yard or garden. This tutorial will guide you through the process of transforming your chain link fence into a beautiful Christmas display. Things You'll Need ----------------- 1. Garland (artificial or real) 2. String lights 3. Zip ties or twist ties 4. Wire cutters 5. Optional decorative items (ribbons, bows, ornaments, etc.) ### Step 1: Choose Your Garland Garland is the foundation of your chain link fence decoration. It creates a lush backdrop that makes other decorations stand out more effectively. Both artificial and real garlands are suitable; however, consider factors such as durability, cost, and environmental impact when making your choice. Artificial garlands tend to be longer-lasting and reusable, while real ones offer a natural scent and appearance. **Key Tip:** Measure the length of your fence before purchasing garland to ensure sufficient coverage. ### Step 2: Attach the Garland to the Fence Use zip ties or twist ties every few feet to securely fasten the garland to the top horizontal rail of the chain link fence. Space these attachments evenly along the entire length of the fence for consistent coverage. Be careful not to pull the garland too tightly against the fence, which could damage its shape and structure. **Guideline:** Aim for gentle curves rather than sharp angles in your garland layout. ### Step 3: Add String Lights Wrap string lights around the garland, intertwining them within the foliage so they remain hidden from view. Start at one end of the fence and work systematically towards the other, ensuring equal spacing between each strand of lights. Use wire cutters if necessary to shorten excessively long cords. **Key Tip:** Opt for LED lights over traditional incandescent bulbs due to their energy efficiency and lower risk of heat damage. ### Step 4: Secure Loose Ends Secure any loose ends of the light strings using additional zip ties or twist ties. Pay special attention to areas where multiple strands meet to prevent sagging or tangling. Remember to leave enough slack near power sources for easy plug-in access. **Guideline:** Test your lights before fully attaching them to avoid wasting time on nonfunctional sections. ### Step 5: Enhance with Additional Decorations Now comes the fun part! Adorn your garland and fencing with extra decorations according to personal preference. Some ideas include large ribbon bows, hanging ornaments, or wreaths attached to individual fence posts. These additions enhance visual interest and create a cohesive theme throughout your outdoor space. **Key Tip:** When adding heavier objects like ornaments, double check the stability of your garland attachment points to avoid accidental detachment during windy conditions. ### Step 6: Turn On & Enjoy Plug in your newly decorated chain link fence and enjoy the fruits of your labor! Don't forget to periodically check all electrical connections throughout the season to maintain safety and functionality. By following this comprehensive tutorial, you have successfully transformed your ordinary chain link fence into a charming Christmas display. Happy decorating! Title: How to Buy a Ukulele Buying a ukulele can be an exciting experience, whether you're a beginner looking for your first instrument or an experienced player searching for a new addition to your collection. This guide will provide you with comprehensive steps and valuable insights to help you make an informed decision when purchasing a ukulele. ### Step 1: Determine Your Budget Before diving into the world of ukuleles, set a budget that aligns with your financial capabilities and musical goals. Ukuleles are available at various price points ranging from $20 to several thousand dollars. A reasonable starting point for beginners is between $50 to $200, which offers decent quality instruments without breaking the bank. Intermediate to advanced players may want to consider investing more for higher-quality sound and build materials. Keep in mind that expensive doesn't always mean better; focus on finding the best value within your means. ### Step 2: Choose the Right Size Ukuleles come in four main sizes: soprano, concert, tenor, and baritone. Each size has its unique characteristics and tonal qualities: 1. **Soprano (standard)** - The smallest and most traditional size, offering bright, cheerful sounds perfect for children and those who prefer high-pitched tones. Its compact size makes it highly portable but might feel cramped for adults with larger hands. * Scale length: ~13 inches * Total Length: ~21 inches 2. **Concert** - Slightly larger than the soprano, providing a slightly deeper tone while maintaining much of the classic ukulele sound. It also features a wider fretboard, making it suitable for musicians with bigger hands. * Scale length: ~15 inches * Total Length: ~23 inches 3. **Tenor** - Bigger still, this type produces a richer, fuller sound closer to that of a guitar. Many professional players favor this size due to its versatile range and comfortable playability. * Scale length: ~17 inches * Total Length: ~26 inches 4. **Baritone** - The largest of the bunch, featuring a deep, resonant sound similar to nylon-string guitars. Baritones typically utilize different tuning compared to smaller ukes (DGBE instead of GCEA), appealing primarily to guitarists seeking a transition instrument or alternative voicings. * Scale length: ~19 inches The story was in XXX and flowers. Hello young mathematicians! Today, we are going to learn about polynomials and their roots. You might already know that a polynomial is just a fancy name for an expression that involves variables and coefficients, and operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, and sometimes even exponentiation. For example, `3x^2 + 2x - 1` is a polynomial. Now, imagine you have a polynomial and you want to find its "roots." What does this mean? A root of a polynomial is simply a value that makes the entire polynomial equal to zero when you substitute it into the polynomial. In other words, if you put the root value in place of the variable, the whole polynomial becomes the number zero. Let me show you an example: Consider the polynomial `p(x) = x^2 - 4`. This polynomial has two roots because there are two values that make it zero. These values are -2 and 2. When you replace `x` with -2 or 2 in the polynomial, you get zero: * p(-2) = (-2)^2 - 4 = 0 * p(2) = 2^2 - 4 = 0 Now, here comes an interesting fact! Did you know that a polynomial of degree `n` can have at most `n` roots? That means a first-degree polynomial (a line) can cross the x-axis at most once, while a second-degree polynomial (a parabola) can touch or cross the x-axis at most twice, and so on. Check out these images illustrating this concept: [Insert image of a line crossing the x-axis once and a parabola touching or crossing the x-axis twice] This idea holds true not only for real numbers but also for complex numbers and many other mathematical structures. It's quite fascinating, isn't it?! Now, go ahead and explore some more polynomials and see if you can discover their roots! Happy mathing! Look how high is the Prudential Tower is. Title: Understanding Risks using Simple Decision Making Hi there! Today, let's learn about making decisions when there are risks involved, just like when a doctor diagnoses a patient. Imagine you have a box full of red and blue marbles. Your task is to guess whether a marble drawn randomly will be red or blue, but here's the twist - you'll face some consequences (risks) depending on your choice. Let's see how we can make smart choices while dealing with these risks. 1. **Seeing the Big Picture:** First, it helps to know all the possible outcomes. In our marble game, imagine choosing "red" each time; sometimes you may be correct, other times wrong. Write down both results to get ready for decision-making. 2. **Counting Marbles:** Next, find out how many red and blue marbles exist. You could count them individually or group together same colored ones, then tally up totals. This step resembles counting data points or probabilities in more complex situations. 3. **Thinking Ahead:** Now, picture the costs associated with being incorrect. Say drawing a red marble gets you one point, but losing five points occurs for any blue draw. The difference between gaining a single point versus losing five shows us the impact of errors. 4. **Smart Choices:** Finally, put everything together! Compare the chance of getting things right against potential losses. It means multiplying the likelihood of success by gain and failure by loss. Choose the option with higher value - meaning less overall risk. So, remember, understanding risks doesn't need to be scary! By breaking down various elements and comparing possibilities, even young minds can tackle big problems efficiently. Just like knowing the odds of having different color marbles, real life scenarios often hide no bigger mystery than that! Do you want to put beans with the melon? Some countries have a constitutional court. These courts decide if a law is in agreement with the constitution of the country. She was holding her hand and everything. Title: How to Buy Furniture from IKEA Place Using Your iPhone or iPad Introduction: IKEA Place is an augmented reality (AR) app that allows you to visualize IKEA products in your own space before making a purchase. This tutorial will guide you through the process of buying furniture using the IKEA Place app on your iPhone or iPad. Step 1: Download and Install IKEA Place Visit the App Store on your iPhone or iPad, search for "IKEA Place," and install the application. Make sure your device meets the minimum requirements for AR functionality. For iOS devices, this typically means having an A9 chip or later. Step 2: Open the IKEA Place App Tap the IKEA Place icon on your home screen or app library to open the app. Familiarize yourself with the interface, which includes categories, a search bar, and a camera button for scanning surfaces. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Keep your device's operating system up-to-date to ensure optimal performance and compatibility with the IKEA Place app. - Explore different product categories by tapping the category buttons at the bottom of the screen. Step 3: Browse Products Scroll through the available products or use the search function to find specific items. You can also filter results based on various criteria such as room type, color, and price range. Once you have found an item of interest, tap its image to view more details. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Read the product description carefully, paying attention to dimensions, materials, and other relevant information. - Check customer reviews and ratings for additional insights into the product's quality and usability. Step 4: Visualize the Product with Augmented Reality To see how the product would look in your space, tap the "Place in my Room" button. The app will then prompt you to scan your surroundings using your device's camera. Follow the on-screen instructions to move your device around until the entire floor area is covered in blue lines. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Find a well-lit area with minimal glare for accurate placement and color representation. - Clear any clutter from the area where you plan to place the virtual furniture to avoid distractions during the visualization process. Step 5: Position the Virtual Furniture Use two fingers to rotate, scale, and adjust the position of the virtual furniture within your space. Adjustments are made in real time, so experiment with different placements until you find one that works best for your needs. When satisfied with the positioning, tap the checkmark button. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Consider factors like traffic flow, natural light, and proximity to power outlets when placing virtual furniture. - Be mindful of scale; make sure the virtual furniture appears proportionate to nearby objects and architectural features. Step 6: Add to Cart After confirming the product's placement, tap the cart icon located near the top right corner of the screen. Review your selection, enter the necessary shipping information, and proceed to checkout. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Double-check all measurements and consider delivery options if purchasing larger pieces of furniture. - Take advantage of promotions, discount codes, and loyalty rewards whenever possible. Conclusion: By following these steps, you should now be able to successfully buy furniture from IKEA Place using your iPhone or iPad. Enjoy exploring the vast array of products offered by IKEA while taking advantage of the convenience and accuracy provided by their innovative AR technology. Happy shopping! Title: Adding Columns in Google Sheets on Android: A Comprehensive Tutorial Introduction: Google Sheets is a powerful tool for organizing, analyzing, and sharing data. This tutorial will guide you through adding columns in Google Sheets on your Android device with clear and easy-to-follow steps. By learning this skill, you'll be able to efficiently manage and expand your spreadsheets as needed. Step 1: Launch Google Sheets app Locate the Google Sheets icon (a multicolored grid) on your Android device and tap it to open the application. If you are not already signed in, enter your Google account credentials to proceed. Key tip: Make sure you have updated the Google Sheets app from the Play Store to enjoy the latest features and improvements. Step 2: Open an existing spreadsheet or create a new one Tap on 'Files' at the bottom left corner of the screen. Choose either 'Open sheet' if you want to work on an existing file or 'Create sheet' to start a new project. For this tutorial, we assume that you chose an existing file. Guideline: Familiarize yourself with the primary elements of the user interface - the top menu bar, column letters, row numbers, cell addresses, and formatting options. Step 3: Navigate to where you want to add a column Using your finger, scroll horizontally until you reach the spot where you wish to insert a new column. Keep track of the letter corresponding to the column immediately next to where you intend to place the new column. Key tip: Pay attention to any data within the current view; adding a column might affect the distribution and organization of information in adjacent cells. Step 4: Tap the three dots ('More') button above the column header Above the right side of the alphabetical headers, find the vertical ellipsis symbol ('More'). Tapping here will reveal several functions related to managing rows and columns. Step 5: Select 'Insert column left' or 'Insert column right' From the dropdown list that appears after tapping the three dots ('More'), choose between 'Insert column left' or 'Insert column right'. The former adds a blank column before the selected location, while the latter places it afterward. For example, selecting 'Insert column left' when positioned over column D would result in a fresh column being placed between C and D, making the original column D shift one position further towards Z. Expert insight: Choosing which option to use depends on whether you need the new column closer to the beginning or end of your dataset. Plan ahead by visualizing the impact of insertion points based on your intended purposes. Step 6: Verify successful addition of the column After completing Step 5, observe the appearance of a newly added column. It should now display near the area where you initially wanted it. Double-check the column label (letter), ensuring it matches the correct placement. Congratulations! You have successfully learned how to add a column in Google Sheets using your Android device. With practice, this process will become second nature, enabling efficient manipulation and management of complex datasets. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/10/5365DL.cha = = = That could be like a chimney, huh? A response to the question: What is the best example or way to introduce the concept of multiplying by 10's, 100's, and 1000's. I am a new teacher and want to make sure my students see the pattern and understand. Can you give me any advice?------------- Seeing the pattern is so important. I am glad you are going that route. What I do when I want my students to see the pattern, or relationship, is to structure an activity that will give them a chance to collect a lot of data that will demonstrate the pattern. Then I let them "notice" it... sometimes with my gentle nudging, of course. In this case, I would have them suggest numbers they could multiply by 10, 100, or 1000, and then try it out with a calculator or by hand. Don't tell them there is a shortcut, just let them do the math and collect the products in a chart of some sort. You could even just write all the products on the board, haphazardly, and let them notice that some have one zero, some two, and some three (be sure that the other factor is a non-multiple of ten at first). Let them try to figure out why that is happening... this zero at the end phenomenon. Their conjectures about what is causing the end zeroes will help you know what questions to ask, getting them to explain what they are thinking will help, too. Then it is a simple problem to form a generalization for the situation. "What happens when we multiply by a multiple of ten is..." Again, I would let THEM be the ones to verbalize the generalization. You are there just to see if it is true, to clarify any mis-comprehension, and to help refine the language. If you haven't ever experienced the power of discovery learning, you are in for a real treat. :-) P.S. A wonderful extension is to wonder aloud (and thus, encourage the students to, also) about what happens if the multiple of ten is something like 20, or 200, or 2000 or ... -Gail, for the T2T service Join a discussion of this topic in T2T. Synesthetes often say that they did not know their experiences were unusual until they found out that other people did not have them. Others report feeling as if they had been keeping a secret their entire lives. Most synesthetes consider their experiences a gift-a "hidden" sense. Most synesthetes find out in their childhood that they have synethesia. Some learn to apply it in daily life and work. For example, they might use their gift to memorize names and telephone numbers or do mental arithmetic. Many people with synesthesia use their experiences to help them be more creative, for example, in making drawings and music. What do you think of Ann More? No you going to have something to drink? People read for two main reasons: Entertainment and information. However, neither of these things can happen if people don't understand and comprehend what they are reading. In this lesson, we will discover how story mapping can help us summarize the main points of a book or article in a diagram that is easy to see and comprehend, thus helping us meet our Materials: A copy of the factual book Pigs, Pigs; informational books on several different kinds of animals (at least one for every child); paper; pencils; marker- or chalkboard; markers or chalk. Procedure: 1) "Good morning, class! Today we are going to talk about things we can do to help us understand stories that we read so we can learn from them. Does anyone know what a main idea is? (Allow time for answers.) That's right; a main idea is what the story is all about. The main idea is usually just a few words or a sentence; you might think of a title as the main idea of a story. After finding the main idea of a story, we should look for the parts of the story that are most important in order for us to understand what the story is about. We are going to learn how to make a story map today that will show us the main idea and the most important parts of the story. 2) "I am going to read the book Pigs, Pigs to you, and we are going to talk about what we learned in the story. (Read Pigs, Pigs.) Let's talk about some of the things we learned from this book. (Let the children discuss what they learned.) Now, I'm going go to the board, and we are going to organize the information we learned by making a story map. First, I'm going to write the main idea in the middle and circle it. What do you think our main idea would be? What was our story all about? (Children will probably say "Pigs!") Yes, it was pigs. So, I'll write Pigs in the middle and circle it. Now, what we want to do next is think about some of the main things we learned about pigs. I'll show you what I mean by doing the first one. First, we learned about pigs' birth. So, I'll draw a line from our main idea, and, at the end of the line, I'll write birth and circle it. Now, we need to list some things we learned about pigs' birth. For each thing we list, we'll draw a line from birth and write and circle each thing we learned about that. I know we learned that pigs are born live from their mother. So, I'll write that and circle it after I draw a line from birth. Now, what are some other things we learned about pigs' birth, how they are born, etc.? (Continue with this topic, then move on when they are out of answers.) Now, let's talk about another important thing we learned about pigs and do the same thing. Can you tell me something else we learned about pigs? Remember, give me a main topic first; then we'll list the details. (Answers will vary, and may include breeds, marketing, where they live, what they eat, how mothers care for babies, etc.) (Continue until all main ideas have been covered.) Great job, class! Now, I can look at this model, and remember all the important facts we learned about pigs. In fact, even if a new student came in who didn't get to hear the book being read, they could probably look at our story map and learn just about everything we did from the book." 3) "Now, I am going to call you up by rows and let you each choose one of these informational books about animals. You may choose whichever one you wish. I want you to go back to your seat and read the book. Then, I'm going to give you a big sheet of paper and a pencil, and I want you to make a story map of your book like the one we made on the board. Remember to first write the main idea, which may just be the animal you read about, then write the most important points or things you learned, then write the details about each one. If you need my help, just raise your hand and I'll help you." 4) For assessment, have the students share their story maps with the class. Have them point to the words on their map and read them aloud. Make sure the students have grasped the main idea, important points, and details in a clear way, so that the audience can get the main points of the book without having read it. Reference: Pressley, Michael. "Strategies That Improve Children's Memory and Comprehension of Text."The Elementary School Journal. Volume 90, Number 1. 1989. Step 2: If using the numbers or story time page line up large bubble wrap on top of the page and tape it together. Step 3: For Story Time Bubble Wrap have the child create a story verbally using the picture icons in any order. When they use the picture in the story they should pop that bubble. Continuing adding parts to your story and popping the bubble wrap until all pictures have been popped. For Number Bubble Wrap you could match this up with other activities such as exercises. If you have those activity books, print out the cards and place them face down. Turn a card over and pop a number bubble. The child can perform the exercise the number of times under the popped bubble wrap. Continue until all the numbers have been popped. For the blank bubble wrap you could write anything such as: sight words, math problems, letters, vocabulary words and more. The child must read the word or do the math problem. If they get it right they can pop the bubble. Continue until all the bubbles are popped. A nickname is a special name that a person may be called that is different from their real name or from their birth name. It is not a legal thing; it is a social thing. The name may be given to them by their friends or family. It may be like the person's real name or completely different. Nicknames often come from how a person looks, or from something they are especially known for doing. They can also be a shortened version of the given name. D' you know who that USTA belong to? Entering multiple lines of text in Slack using an iPhone or iPad is a straightforward process that can be accomplished by following these steps: 1. Open the Slack app on your iPhone or iPad. The first thing you need to do is locate the Slack icon (a blue bubble with a white 'S') on your device's home screen or app library and tap on it to open the app. This will take you to the main screen where you can access all of your channels and direct messages. 2. Navigate to the channel or conversation where you want to enter multiple lines of text. You can do this by tapping on the appropriate name from the list of channels or conversations displayed on the left-hand side of the screen. If you don't see the channel or conversation you're looking for, you can search for it by typing its name into the search bar at the top of the screen. 3. Tap the message input field. Once you have navigated to the correct channel or conversation, look for the message input field at the bottom of the screen. It should say something like "Type a message" followed by a cursor. Tap on this field to begin entering your text. 4. Enable line breaks. By default, when you hit the return or enter key on your keyboard while composing a message in Slack, it sends the message instead of creating a new line. To enable line breaks, you need to press and hold down the return or enter key until a popup appears with several options, including "Return." Release your finger once the "Return" option is highlighted, and this will create a new line without sending the message. 5. Type your first line of text. Now that you have enabled line breaks, go ahead and type the first line of your message. Make sure to leave enough space between the end of your sentence and the next line so that it is easy to read. 6. Repeat step 5 as needed. Continue pressing and holding the return or enter key to create new lines and then typing out each subsequent line of your message. Keep in mind that there may be character limits depending on whether you are posting in a public channel or private group. 7. Add formatting if necessary. While typing your multi-line message, you might find it helpful to add some basic formatting such as bold, italics, or bullet points to make your content more engaging. To do this, simply highlight the relevant words or phrases within your message and select the desired format option from the menu above the keyboard. 8. Preview your message before sending. Before hitting send, take a moment to review your multi-line message for any errors or typos. Since you cannot edit sent messages in Slack, it's essential to ensure everything looks right before submitting it. 9. Send your message. When you are satisfied with your multi-line message, tap the blue arrow button located on the far right of the input field. This action will post your message to the selected channel or conversation, displaying each line neatly spaced apart. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Remember that enabling line breaks requires you to press and hold the return or enter key until a popup appears. Simply tapping the key won't work. * Be aware of character limits in different channels and groups. Exceeding them could result in truncation or errors during posting. * Always proofread your message before sending it to avoid embarrassing mistakes. * Consider adding formatting to enhance readability and engagement. * Practice good etiquette by keeping your messages clear, concise, and respectful. Albinism is a hereditary condition. It is usually inherited in a recessive pattern; it means, both parents have to give the albinism gene to a child to cause albinism. Parents pass on pairs of genes to their children. One of the pairs of genes is in charge of making melanin. If both of these genes are flawed, then little or no pigment is made. If only one gene is flawed then pigment can still be made. And how did you first hear about Feet man? We also have some sweets over here. Finding Nemo is a 2003 American computer-animated comedy adventure film written and directed by Andrew Stanton, released by Walt Disney Pictures, and the fifth film produced by Pixar Animation Studios. It tells the story of the over-protective clownfish named Marlin (Albert Brooks) who, along with a regal blue tang named Dory (Ellen DeGeneres), searches for his captured son Nemo (Alexander Gould) in Sydney Harbour. Along the way, Marlin learns to take risks and let Nemo take care of himself. It is Pixar's first film to be released in cinemas in the northern hemisphere summer. The film was re-released for the first time in 3D on September 14, 2012, and it was released on Blu-ray on December 4, 2012. A sequel, "Finding Dory", was released on June 17, 2016. Plug the given t-values into the given formula santaclaus wrote:1. Let t be in seconds and let r(t) be the rate< in gallons/second, at which water enters a reservoir. We are given that, for t 0<t<30,: r(t) = 800 - 40t 1. evaluate the expressions r(0), r(15), r(25) and explain their physical significance. , and simplify to find the r-values. Keeping in mind that "t" is defined as the time in seconds and "r" is defined as the rate of flow in gallons per second, state the r- and t-value pairs in terms of rate of flow r at a given time t. For instance, the value of r(5) would be the rate of flow, in gallons per second, at a time of five seconds. santaclaus wrote:2. if you were to graph y=r(t) for 0<t<30, labeling the intercepts. What is the physical significance of the slope and the intercepts? To learn about the meaning of slope and y-intercept in the context of word problems, try here (well, okay; the t-intercept) is of course when r (usually y) equals zero. What does "r" stand for? What does "t" stand for? Interpret this intercept in terms of the rate of flow at that time t. santaclaus wrote:3. for 0<t<30, when does the reservoir have the most water? When does it have the least water? Look at the graph . When (at what t-value) is the line the highest? When is it the lowest? santaclaus wrote:4. What are the domain and range of r? They gave you the domain when they gave you the restrictions on t. To find the range , find the output values over the interval for which the function is defined. Yes, "y = 800 - 40x" is defined "everywhere", but that isn't the domain they gave you. And yes, "y = 800 - 40x" goes "everywhere" (eventually attains every possible y-value), but that won't happen over the domain they gave you. So use the highest and lowest values from part (3) to state the range for r(t). If you get stuck, please reply showing your work and reasoning so far. Thank you! A sequence is an ordered list of numbers: 3, 6, 9, 12, .... 1, 3, 5, 7, ... A sequence can be thought of as a list of numbers written in a definite order: a1, a2, a3, ..., an, ... The number a1 is called the first term, a2 is the second term, and in general an is the nth term. The terms of a sequence often follow a particular pattern. In those instances, we can determine the general term that expresses every term of the sequence. For example: Given an=3n+2, find a1, a2, a3 and a4. By substituting n=1,2,3,4 in the general term of the sequence, we obtain: Find the general term for the following sequences: This time the bees and the frogs the snails and the rocks. Title: How to Fix Sony Vegas File I/O Error When Loading Sony Vegas is a popular video editing software used by many professionals and amateurs alike. However, one common issue that users encounter is the File I/O error, which prevents them from loading their projects. This tutorial will provide you with an in-depth guide on how to fix this problem, ensuring that your creativity isn't hindered by technical difficulties. **Step 1: Understanding the File I/O Error** File Input/Output (I/O) errors usually occur due to issues related to data transfer between the hard drive and the application. It could be caused by corrupt files, insufficient system resources, or problems within the application itself. Key Tip: Always save multiple copies of your project file at different stages to prevent loss of work. **Step 2: Restart Your Computer** Before diving into complex solutions, try restarting your computer. Sometimes, simple restarts can clear up temporary glitches causing the File I/O error. Guideline: Save any open work before restarting. **Step 3: Check Your Hard Drive** Ensure your hard drive has enough space and is functioning correctly. You can do this by running disk check utility on Windows or Disk Utility on MacOS. Key Tip: If possible, back up your important data regularly to avoid data loss during these checks. **Step 4: Reinstall Sony Vegas** Uninstall and then reinstall Sony Vegas. Uninstallation removes all associated files, while reinstallation ensures you have the latest version without corrupted files. Guideline: Make sure to download the installer from a trusted source like the official Sony website. **Step 5: Disable Antivirus Software Temporarily** Antivirus software sometimes interferes with normal operations of applications. Try disabling it temporarily while opening your project in Sony Vegas. Remember to enable it once you are done. Key Tip: Only disable antivirus when necessary as it leaves your system vulnerable to threats. **Step 6: Repair Corrupt Files** If the problem persists, there might be something wrong with your project file. First, make a backup copy of the file. Then, try repairing it using third-party tools designed specifically for this purpose. Guideline: Be careful when choosing such tools; ensure they are reputable and compatible with your operating system and file format. **Step 7: Contact Sony Support** If none of the above steps worked, consider reaching out to Sony's customer support. They may provide specific troubleshooting advice based on your situation or even offer a solution directly. Remember, fixing technical issues often requires patience and perseverance. Don't give up if the first few methods don't yield results immediately. With determination and help from this guide, you should be able to resolve the File I/O error and get back to creating fantastic videos in no time! Oh, what's in there, ah [breathes in]. I didn't say mm I said uhh. You height wait until eight o clock. Activity 5: Earthquake Waves SURFING ROCK WAVES Earthquakes happen all over the world. On the surface we do not always feel them. The movement we do feel can be described in two motions: P waves and S waves. Students will use slinkies to imitate and better understand the two types of waves. Instructional Method: Experiment Goal: Explain wave motions associated with earthquakes. Objectives: Students will be able to: Activity time: 30 minutes When movement occurs between two pieces of crust, called fault blocks, we feel an earthquake. To create a mini-earthquake, slide two blocks of rock against each other. Can you feel the roughness of the two rocks as they pass by each other? The friction between the two rocks is what creates the vibrations in an earthquake except on a much greater scale. These vibrations in nature are called seismic waves. Most earthquakes are too small to be felt by people. Larger earthquakes are not only felt by people, but can severely damage structures. The motion from an earthquake travels through rock in the form of waves. There are two types of waves associated with earthquakes: P waves and S waves. The fastest of the waves is a primary wave, or P wave. These waves feel like a push-and-pull movement on the crust as it is stretched and compressed. P waves are also called compressional waves. The slower of the two waves is called a secondary wave, or S wave. These waves travel in a side to side motion and produce more shaking than P waves. The side-to-side motion of S-waves causes the most damage to structures during an earthquake. In large earthquakes it is possible to feel the difference between P and S waves. P waves feel like someone has run a semi-truck into your house and S waves feel more like you are riding waves at a lake or ocean. The following schematic shows how these waves travel through rock. It is possible to simulate the motion of both P and S waves by using a Slinky. The push-pull motion of a P wave can be seen by stretching and compressing a slinky. The shaking motion of an S wave can be seen by moving a stretched Slinky side to side. Have you ever experienced an earthquake? What did it feel like to you? Could you distinguish between the P waves and the S waves? Which do you think is more destructive to houses, P waves or S waves? Why? What do you think you should do to protect yourself during an earthquake? Instead of using slinkies you can create P and S waves in a box of sand. Fill a rectangular box with 2-3 inches of sand. Be sure the sand is very dry. On the sand place small sticks or Lego houses. Shake the box or hit the side of it and observe what happens to the house and sand. It may help to place toothpick markers throughout the sand standing on end. Watch what happens to the toothpicks when the box is shaken. Does this work? Can you distinguish between P and S waves? How? Do you have to shake the box differently to create both types of waves? Sun WWW [coughs] came up and pop out of the egg came a tiny and very hungry caterpillar. Sure! Let's talk about how we can organize groups of things using something called "Cartesian products." This concept is named after French mathematician Ren Descartes and helps us combine sets of items together. Imagine you have two bags filled with different colored balls - Bag A has red balls numbered 1 through 5, while Bag B contains blue balls labeled 1 to 3. Now, let's create a new group by combining each ball from Bag A with every ball from Bag B. We call this the Cartesian product of Bag A and Bag B, written as Bag A Bag B. To visualize this, think of creating a table where each row represents a ball from Bag A and each column stands for a ball from Bag B. The intersection points will contain the combined pair: | Red Ball # / Blue Ball # | 1 | 2 | 3 | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | 1 | {(1, 1)} | {(1, 2)} | {(1, 3)} | | 2 | {(2, 1)} | {(2, 2)} | {(2, 3)} | | 3 | {(3, 1)} | {(3, 2)} | {(3, 3)} | | 4 | {(4, 1)} | {(4, 2)} | {(4, 3)} | | 5 | {(5, 1)} | {(5, 2)} | {(5, 3)} | The notation "(a, b)" indicates the pair containing item 'a' and 'b'. In our example, the top left cell shows the first red ball paired with the first blue ball, represented as (1, 1). Similarly, the last entry on the bottom right corner displays the fifth red ball matched with the third blue ball, which looks like (5, 3). In mathematics, when dealing with natural numbers instead of ball bags, we may see notations like N or $\mathbb{N}$, representing the set of natural numbers starting from 1. So, writing N x N suggests combining every possible pair of natural numbers together, similar to how we created the Cartesian product between Bag A and Bag B earlier. Now, back to your original question regarding expressing statements clearly - imagine you want to convey the idea of three variables (k, l, m), which must satisfy certain conditions within the context of N x N x N. You could say: Consider any three whole numbers k, l, and m taken from the collection formed by multiplying N together three times, under these rules: * k should be greater than 1; * l should be larger than 2; * m must be more significant than both k + l added together. This way, even young learners without advanced math skills can understand the meaning behind complex mathematical expressions! let's see what's in the book. The goal is to maintain a normal blood glucose level. The normal range for blood glucose is 80-120 mg/dL (milligrams of glucose per deciliter of blood) or 3.5-7 mmol/l (millimole per liter of blood). These are different ways of saying the same thing, much like yards and meters are different units of distance measurement. Central Limit Theorem Introduction to the central limit theorem and the sampling distribution of the mean Central Limit Theorem Use this menu to view and help create subtitles for this video in many different languages. You'll probably want to hide YouTube's captions if using these subtitles. - In this video I want to talk about what is easily one of the - most fundamental and profound concepts in statistics and - maybe in all of mathematics. - And that's the central limit theorem. - And what it tells us is we could start off with any - distribution that has a well-defined mean and variance. - And if it has a well-defined variance, it has a well-defined - standard deviation. - And it can be a continuous distribution or a discrete one. - I'll draw a discrete one just because it's easier to - imagine at least for the purposes of this video. - So let's say I have a discreet probability - distribution function. - And I want to be very careful not to make it look anything - close to a normal distribution because I want to show you the - power of the central limit theorem. - So let's say I have a distribution. - Let's say it could take on values 1 through - 6: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. - It's some kind of crazy dice. - It's very likely to get a 1, let's say it's impossible-- let - me make that a straight line-- you've a very high likelihood - of getting a 1, let's say it's impossible to get a 2, let's - say it's an OK likelihood of getting a 3 or a 4. - Let's say it's impossible get a 5. - And let's say it's very likely to get a 6 like that. - So that's my probability distribution function. - If I were to draw a mean, this is symmetric, so maybe the mean - would be something like that. - The mean would be halfway. - So that would be my mean right there. - The standard deviation maybe it would look-- it'd be - that far and that far above and below the mean. - But that's my discreet probability - distribution function. - Now what I'm going to do here, instead of just taking samples - of this random variable that's described by this probability - distribution function, I'm going to take samples of it. - But I'm going to average the samples and then look at those - samples and see the frequency of the averages that I get. - And when I say average I mean the mean. - So let's say-- and let me define something-- let's say my - sample size, and I could put any number here, but let's say - first off we try a sample size of n is equal to 4. - from your distribution. - But the terminology can be very confusing because you can - easily view one of these as a sample. - But we're taking 4 samples from here. - We have a sample size of 4. - And what I'm going to do is I'm going to average them. - So let's say the mean-- I'm going to be very careful when I - say average-- the mean of this first sample of size 4 is what? - 1 plus 1 is 2. - 2 plus 3 is 5. - 5 plus 6 is 11. - 11 divided by 4 is 2.75. - That is my first sample mean for my first sample of size 4. - Let me do another one. - My second sample of size 4. - Let's say that I get a 3, a 4, let's say I get another 3, - and let's say I get a 1. - I just didn't happen to get a 6 that time. - And notice I can't get a 2 or a 5. - That's impossible for this distribution. - The chance of getting a 2 or a 5 is zero. - So I can't have any 2's or 5's over here. - So for this second sample of sample size 4, my sample mean-- - so my second sample mean is going to be 3 plus 4 is 7. - 7 plus 3 is 10 plus 1 is 11. - 11 divided by 4 once again is 2.75. - Let me do one more because I really want to make it clear - what we're doing here. - So I do one more-- actually we're going to do a gazillion - more, but let me just do one more in detail. - So let's say my third sample of sample size 4 I get-- some I'm - going to literally takes 4 samples. - So my sample is made up of 4 samples from this original - crazy distribution. - Let's say I get a 1, a 1, a 6 and a 6. - And so my third sample mean is going to be 1 plus 1 is 2. - 2 plus 6 is 8. - 8 plus 6 is 14. - 14 divided by 4 is 3.5. - And as I find each of these sample means-- so for each of - my samples of sample size 4 I figure out a mean-- and as I do - each of them I'm going to plot it on a frequency distribution. - And this is all going to amaze you in a few seconds. - So I plot this all on a frequency distribution. - So I say, OK, on my first sample my first - sample mean was 2.75. - So I'm plotting the actual frequency of the sample means - I get for each sample. - So 2.75, I got it one time. - So I'll put a little plot there. - So that's from that one right there. - And the next time I also got a 2.75. - That's a 2.75 there. - So I got it twice. - So I'll plot the frequency right there. - Then I got a 3.5. - So all the possible values, I could have a 3, I could have - a 3.25, I could have a 3.5. - So then I had the 3.5 so I'll plot it right there. - And what I'm going to do is I'm going to keep - taking these samples. - Maybe I'll take 10,000 of them. - So I'm going to keep taking these samples. - So I go all the way to s 10,000. - I just do a bunch of these. - And what it's going to look like over time is each of these - I'm going to make a dot because I'm going to have to zoom out. - So if I look at it like this, over time, it still has all - the values that it might be able to take on. - You know, 2.75 might be here. - So this first dot is going to be this one right here is going - to be right there and that second one is going to be right - there and then that one at 3.5 is going to look right there. - But I'm going to do it 10,000 times so I'm - going to have 10,000. - And let's say as I do it, I'm going to just - keep plotting them. - I'm just going to keep plotting the frequencies. - I'm just going to keep plotting them over and - over and over again. - And what you're going to see is as I take many, many - samples of size 4. - I'm going to have something that's going to start - kind of approximating a normal distribution. - So each of these dots represent an incidence of a sample mean. - So as I keep adding on this column right here that means - I kept getting the sample mean 2.75. - So over time I'm going to have something that's starting to - approximate a normal distribution. - And that is a neat thing about the central limit theorem. - So the central limit-- and this was the case for-- so in - orange, that's the case for n is equal to 4. - This was for sample size of 4. - Now if I did the same thing with a sample size of maybe 20. - So in this case instead of just taking 4 samples from my - original crazy distribution every sample I take 20 - instances of my random variable and I average those 20 and then - I plot the sample mean on here. - So in that case, I'm going to have a distribution - that looks like this. - And we'll discuss this in more videos. - But it turns out if I were to plot 10,000 of the sample means - here, I'm going to have something that-- two things: - it's going to even more closely approximate a normal - And we're going to see in future videos it's actually - going to have a smaller-- well, let me be clear-- it's going - to have the same mean. - So that's the mean. - This is going to have the same mean. - It's going to have a smaller standard deviation. - So I should plot these from the bottom because - you kind of stack it. - One you get 1 and then another instance then another instance. - But this is going to more and more approach a - normal distribution. - So the reality is-- and this is what's super cool about the - central limit theorem-- as your sample size becomes larger, - or you can even say as it approaches infinity, but you - really don't have to get that close to infinity to really get - close to a normal distribution. - Even if you have a sample size of 10 or 20, you're already - getting very close to a normal distribution. - In fact, about as good an approximation as we see - in our everyday life. - But what's cool is we can start with some crazy - distribution, right? - This has nothing to do with a normal distribution. - But if we have a sample size-- this was n equals 4-- but if we - have a sample size of n equals 10 or n equals 100, and we were - to take 100 of these instead of 4 here and average them and - then plot that average, the frequency of it. - Then we take 100 again, average them, take the - mean, plot that again. - And if we were to do that a bunch of times, in fact, if we - were to do that an infinite time, we would find-- - especially if we had an infinite sample size-- we - would find a perfect normal distribution. - That's the crazy thing. - And it doesn't apply just to taking the sample mean. - Here we took the sample mean every time but you could have - also taken the sample sum. - The central limit theorem would have still applied. - But that's what's so super useful about it. - Because in life there's all sorts of processes out there, - proteins bumping into each other, people doing crazy - things, humans interacting in weird ways. - And you don't know the probability distribution - functions for any of those things. - But what the central limit theorem them tells us is if we - add a bunch of those actions together, assuming that they - all have the same distribution, or if we were to take the mean - of all of those actions together and if we were to plot - the frequency of those means, we do get a normal - And that's frankly why the normal distribution shows up so - much in statistics and why frankly it's a very good - approximation for the sum or the means of a lot - of processes. - Normal distribution. - What I'm going to show you in the next video is I'm actually - going to show you that this is a reality. - That as you increase your sample size, as you increase - your n, and as you take a lot of sample means, you're going - to have a frequency plot that looks very, very close to - Mud and Snow, (or M+S, or M&S), is a designation used by manufacturers for all-season and winter tires. Most are designed to provide improved performance under low temperature conditions, compared to summer tires. The tread compound is usually softer than that used in tires for summer conditions. This provides better grip on ice and snow, but wears more quickly at higher temperatures. Tires may have well above average numbers of sipes in the tread pattern to grip the ice. M&S relates to the percentage of tread void area. On four-wheel drive vehicles, M&S tires are often standard equipment. 'Cause we do clap together all the time she loves to clap. Body size of herbivores is a key reason underlying the interaction between herbivores and plant diversity, and the body size explains many of the phenomena connected to herbivore-plant interaction. Small herbivores are less likely to decrease plant diversity. Because small non-digging animals may not cause many disturbances to the plant and the environment. Intermediate-sized herbivores mostly increase plant diversity by consuming or influencing the dominant plant species, such as herbivore birds, that can directly use dominant plant species. While some herbivores enhance plant diversity by indirect effects on plant competition. Some digging animals at this size local community environmental fluctuations. And the adaptation of plant species to avoid predators can also adjust the vegetation structure and increase diversity. Larger herbivores often increase plant diversity. They use competitively dominant plant species, and disperse seeds and create disorder of the soil. Besides, their urine position also adjusts the local plant distribution, and prevent light competition. yeah, he's got duck feet, doesn't he? Who wears a tutu at our house? This guide will provide you with in-depth steps and helpful tips to increase your chances of winning their heart. Remember, every person is unique, so what works for one might not work for another. Patience, persistence, and respect are crucial throughout this process. **Step 1: Know Your Crush** Before making any moves, take time to observe and understand your crush's personality, interests, values, and habits. This will help you connect with them on a deeper level and show that you genuinely care about who they are as a person. *Key Tip:* Avoid making assumptions based solely on appearances or stereotypes. Get to know them authentically. **Step 2: Boost Your Confidence** Confidence attracts people. Work on building your self-esteem by focusing on your strengths and accomplishments. Practice good posture, maintain eye contact during conversations, and speak clearly and assertively. *Guideline:* Confidence doesn't mean being arrogant; it means believing in yourself and appreciating your worth. **Step 3: Improve Your Hygiene and Appearance** First impressions matter, and good hygiene shows respect for both yourself and others. Shower daily, brush your teeth, wear clean clothes, and use deodorant. Consider upgrading your wardrobe to reflect current styles and trends while staying true to yourself. *Key Tip:* Dress appropriately for school settings while still expressing your personal style. **Step 4: Engage in Meaningful Conversations** Start conversations about shared interests, school events, or relevant news topics. Ask open-ended questions to encourage dialogue and listen actively when they respond. Show genuine interest in getting to know them better. *Guideline:* Avoid controversial subjects like politics or religion early on. Save those discussions for later once trust has been established. **Step 5: Be Supportive and Kind** Offer kind words and gestures whenever possible. Compliment them sincerely, empathize with their struggles, and celebrate their achievements. By demonstrating kindness and support, you build trust and deepen connections. *Key Tip:* Make sure compliments are genuine and specific - avoid generic praise. **Step 6: Develop Common Interests** Find activities or hobbies that you both enjoy and participate together. Shared experiences create memories and strengthen bonds between individuals. *Guideline:* Don't force an interest just to spend time with them. Authentic connections require mutual passions. **Step 7: Display Honesty and Integrity** Be truthful in all interactions and demonstrate consistency between actions and words. Trust is essential in any relationship, especially at its beginning stages. *Key Tip:* Honesty isn't always easy, but it fosters respect and authenticity. **Step 8: Express Your Feelings Gradually** Once you feel comfortable and confident in your connection, gently express your feelings towards them. Choose a private setting where they won't feel pressured or embarrassed. Respect their response and give them space if needed. *Guideline:* Rejection may happen; remember to remain respectful and gracious regardless of the outcome. Title: How to Change the Position of the Wbar Dock in AV Linux Introduction: AV Linux is a popular multimedia-centric distribution based on Debian's Stable branch. It comes with an elegant wbar dock located at the bottom of the screen by default. However, some users might prefer changing its position according to their workflow or personal preference. This tutorial will guide you through the process of moving the wbar dock to different positions on your desktop. Growing Independence and Fluency In order to become better readers, students must learn to read fluently. Reading with fluency is being able to read with automatic word recognition, which results in the ability to read text at a quick, smooth rate, and with expression. Fluency helps students comprehend what they are reading since fluent readers are more focused on what the words mean then how to pronounce them. Rereading is one strategy that this lesson will use to help with fluency that. Rereading to create fluency really helps children to understand the meaning of the text. stopwatch (to keep up with one-minute reads) Fun in the Woods decodable text, worksheet with words read per minute. 1. Begin the lesson by explaining to the students the importance of fluency. Read the sentence , "Hiking with my family on a nice fall day is fun" (Stopping to sound out each word) Ask the students if they understood what you just read. Now re read the sentence with smoothness and enthusiasm. Talk about how important reading with fluency is and how it helps us understand more of what we are reading. Explain how rereading the same text helps us to better comprehend what we read. "Does anyone know the saying "practice makes perfect?" well today we are going to prove that it is true by rereading a book. When we reread books it helps us read quicker and smoother and helps us understand more of what we read. It helps us gain fluency" 2. Model how to read a passage with more expression from Fun in the Hills. "I am going to read a sentence to you in two different ways. When I finish, I want you to tell me which way you liked the best." First read in a very monotone voice pausing to sound out individual letters. Then read it again in an expressive, smooth voice. Sam and Ted ran and ran. They did not look back. They did not want to see. So, which way did you like best? I liked the second way the best too! Can you raise your hand and tell me something I did differently on the second reading?" Have students discuss what made the second reading sound better than the first. Another tool we can use is crosschecking. Cross checking is a tool that fluent readers use to make sense of the sentences that they read and to read more smoothly. It reminds us to go back and re read what we do not understand until it makes sense. 3. "Now it's your turn to practice fluent reading." Put students in groups of two. "With your partner, you will listen to them read one time through Fun in the Woods in one minute. Once one-minute is up, you will record the number of words you read in one-minute on the words read per minute worksheet. 4. After all students are finished reading the story through give them further instructions, "You will do the same thing for the second and third time you read the story. Your partner will also be using the checklist to see each time you read if you remembered more words, read faster, smoother, or with more expression. Once you have read 3 times and your partner has recorded all the information for the checklist and the words read per minute worksheet, then you will switch and record the information for them." This guide will walk you through the process of enabling this feature using Ubuntu's Unity Tweak Tool. **Step 1: Update Your System** Before making any changes, ensure that your system is up-to-date. Open Terminal (Ctrl+Alt+T) and enter the following commands one by one: ```sql sudo apt update sudo apt upgrade ``` This ensures that all installed packages are at their latest versions, improving stability and security. **Step 2: Install Unity Tweak Tool** Unity Tweak Tool provides easy access to customize various aspects of the Unity desktop environment including auto-hide settings for the launcher. To install it, type the following command into Terminal: ``` sudo apt install unity-tweak-tool ``` Confirm the installation by typing 'Y' when prompted. **Step 3: Familiarize Yourself with Unity Tweak Tool** Once installed, open Dash (the top icon on the left sidebar), search for "Unity Tweak Tool" and launch it. Take some time to explore its interface; we'll specifically focus on the "Launcher" section located under the "Appearance" category. **Step 4: Enable Autohide Functionality** In the Launcher tab, find the option labeled "Show the launcher". Change the dropdown menu next to it from "Always visible" to "Autohide". By doing so, the launcher will disappear unless you hover over the edge of the screen or press the Super (Windows) key. **Step 5: Customize Additional Settings (Optional)** There are additional options available here which you might want to configure according to personal preference: - **Reveal Pressure**: Determines how much pressure is required before revealing the launcher after hiding it. A lower value means less force is needed. - **Hide Delay**: Sets the delay before the launcher hides itself once the mouse leaves its area. Increasing this number could prevent accidental hidings. - **Unhide Delay**: Decides how quickly the launcher appears post-revealing. Slower times reduce sudden appearance. Feel free to adjust these values based on comfort and necessity. **Step 6: Test Out The Changes** Minimize Unity Tweak Tool and test out the new autohide functionality. Move your cursor towards the left side of the screen to see if the launcher correctly shows up upon hovering. Try minimizing windows to observe whether the launcher automatically retracts. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Remember, small tweaks go a long way. Don't hesitate to play around with different settings until you find what works best for you. - Always double-check spelling and syntax when entering terminal commands to avoid potential errors. - Should anything go wrong during this process, simply uninstall the Unity Tweak Tool via Software Center or Terminal (sudo apt remove unity-tweak-tool). Restarting should return everything back to normal. What are you giving them to eat? Madison is called the "Father of the Constitution" because he helped write a large part of it and persuaded people that it was a good one. The canopy is like a huge green roof over the forest. The trees grow up thin and straight, like pillars seeking the sun. They do not put out branches until they are very tall. Then, they spread out like an umbrella in the sunlight. The trees are so close together that very little light penetrates through to the lower layers. Many kinds of monkeys, birds, and insects live in the layer. The animals that call this layer home often never touch the ground throughout their entire lives. They find everything they need existing within the canopy. Water can be accessed from the boles of trees, if not, the leaves and epiphytes that grow in the canopy can supply that need. The animals eat the leaves and fruit of the trees, insects, or other animals. The tall canopy trees must be able to reach the sunlight high in the air and still get nutrients from the thin soil on the forest floor. Their roots do not go deep into the soil because there is nothing there for the plants to reach. Instead, the roots spread out in all directions along or just under the ground. That way they can quickly make use of the nutrients from the recycled plants and animals that have fallen to the floor. Now this dolly could go to school okay? A stamp may be used in place of an X. This X represents the number one (1). PRACTICE counting with one, i.e. "one desk", one student". WRITE a large one (1) on the left side of a chalkboard. Have each child MAKE a strip of ten X's, CUT it out, and PASTE it on butcher paper. This row represents the number ten (10). PRACTICE counting with ten, i.e. "ten fingers", "ten pieces of chalk". WRITE a zero (0) next to the one (1) on the board to make a ten (10). Have each group PASTE ten strips of ten X's together to make a block of100 on a sheet of butcher paper. This represents the number one hundred (100). PRACTICE counting with one hundred. WRITE a zero (0) next to the ten (10) on the board to make one hundred (100). Have the students PRACTICE by looking for one, ten, and a hundred in the world around them. EXAMPLE: About how many toes are in the classroom? Ten toes on ten children makes a hundred toes. If desired, this activity could be extended to a thousand, or even to a million. What's Happening? Find out more about powers of ten and pH. a jelly bean counting contest. Fill a large jar with jelly beans and give a prize to the best guesser. How could you guess how many jelly beans there are in a jar? Experiment with looking at works of art together and connecting them using a "big idea," such as places, people, or materials. Find a work of art that interests you both. Don't look for the wall label right away! What can you see in the work of art without any additional information? Explore the galleries to find and discuss works of art that relate to your theme. Even an object that does not seem to relate can start a conversation about why it might not fit. Use a work of art to create an imaginary story based on your theme. See what you can come up with together by looking carefully at the work of art and asking questions. Ask your child to share what they notice about a particular work of art. Questions such as "What colors do you see?" and "What objects are in the picture?" are great conversation starters. You can turn observation into a memory game by seeing how many details you remember when you are not looking at the work of art. Encourage close looking by making a list of the colors, shapes or lines you can find in an work of art. Circle all the elements on your list that you can also find in a different work of art and compare the two. For a work of art that involves people, imagine a conversation between two figures. What might they be saying to each other? Why? Imagine a thought bubble over a single figure. What could they be thinking? For an work of art that depicts a place, imagine what it might be like there. What could you hear, feel or smell in that place? Create a descriptive title for an work of art, then look at the wall label for more information. Compare the information on the label to your initial ideas about the work. Is it made from different materials than you thought? Does the artist's title change your opinion about the work of art? In 1842 she and Emily travelled to Brussels to enroll in a pensionnat (boarding school) run by Constantin Heger (1809 - 1896) and his wife Claire Zo Parent Heger (1814 - 1891). In return for this, Charlotte taught English and Emily taught music. Their time at the pensionnat was cut short when Elizabeth Branwell, their aunt, died in October 1842. Title: How to Check Your Wi-Fi Network's Address on an iPhone Introduction: Knowing your Wi-Fi network's address can be useful when configuring various networking settings or troubleshooting connectivity issues. This article will guide you through checking your Wi-Fi network's IP (Internet Protocol) address using your iPhone. By following these steps, you'll gain valuable information about your home or office network that may help resolve problems or fine-tune configurations. Step 1: Access Settings App Grab your iPhone and locate its native "Settings" app. It has a gray icon with gears inside. Tap the icon to open it, as this application serves as the main hub for customizing all sorts of device preferences. Step 2: Navigate to Wi-Fi Menu Inside the "Settings" app, scroll down until you find the option labeled "Wi-Fi." Tapping this entry will bring up a list of nearby wireless networks along with some additional options related to Wi-Fi management. Make sure Wi-Fi is turned ON before proceeding further. Step 3: Connect to Targeted Wireless Network Ensure that you are connected to the Wi-Fi network whose IP address you wish to determine. Look at the right side of the available Wi-Fi networks; there should be a blue 'i' button next to the name of the connected network. If so, tap that button to continue. Please note that if you do not see the blue 'i', ensure you're connected to the correct network by tapping it once and waiting for confirmation. Step 4: View Network Details After tapping the blue 'i' button, you'll now have access to more granular details regarding the chosen Wi-Fi network. Here, you can view important information such as router, DNS server addresses, IP version (IPv4/IPv6), and most importantly, your local IP address assigned within the context of this particular network. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Remember that the displayed IP address pertains only to your current connection session. Once reconnected or after restarting your device, chances are high that you might receive a different IP address due to dynamic addressing schemes employed by routers. * Keep in mind that public Wi-Fi hotspots often employ carrier-grade NAT technologies, meaning multiple devices share a single public IP address while maintaining private ones internally. Thus, determining a unique public IP via your phone won't necessarily provide meaningful insights into individual user connections. * To copy the obtained IP address, press and hold it until a 'Copy' option appears. Alternatively, perform a standard text selection gesture followed by choosing 'Copy'. Now, you can paste the copied content anywhere needed - notes, messages, emails, etc. Conclusion: Understanding how to check your Wi-Fi network's address on an iPhone provides beneficial knowledge when dealing with various networking scenarios. With these clear-cut instructions and essential background info, we hope you feel confident navigating through your device settings and extracting relevant data whenever required. Happy networking! oh, no, no no that's not a real sippy cup. It's kind of boring said baby bear. I screamed at her too much and the deal and everybody gets to do what they want.
